pathophysiology_summary: Anterior epistaxis arises from the anterior nasal septum (Little's area/Kiesselbach's plexus), a superficial vascular anastomosis vulnerable to dry air, digital/blowing trauma and local inflammation (here allergic rhinitis + dry heating). Most anterior bleeds are benign and controllable with correct first aid: sit up, lean forward (to avoid swallowing blood), and pinch the soft cartilaginous part of the nose continuously for 10–15 minutes. The prolonged bleeding here is largely due to incorrect technique (head tilted back, pinching the bony bridge). The ground-truth task is (1) correcting technique, (2) distinguishing anterior (controllable, visible source) from posterior epistaxis (uncontrolled, bleeding down the throat despite pressure — needs ED), and (3) screening for anticoagulation/coagulopathy and haemodynamic compromise.

differential.evidence_basis: Unilateral bleed after nose-blowing with dry-air/allergic-rhinitis context.
Must exclude — persistent bleeding down the throat despite correct anterior pressure suggests posterior source needing ED.
Screen for anticoagulants/bleeding disorder that would change management.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Anterior epistaxis leads; posterior bleed and coagulopathy must be excluded and correct technique instituted immediately.

differential.evidence_basis: Bleeding stops with correct anterior pressure, no flow down the throat when compressed, no anticoagulant/coagulopathy, haemodynamically stable — benign anterior bleed.
Controlled by anterior pressure; escalation target if it fails.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After correct technique controls bleeding and red flags are negative, benign anterior epistaxis leads; home care with escalation if uncontrolled.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; benign, controllable; ED if uncontrolled >20 min or haemodynamic compromise.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign controllable anterior epistaxis.

red_flags.description: Posterior bleed — bleeding not controlled by correct anterior pressure, or continuous flow down the throat
red_flags.clinical_significance: Posterior bleeds can be brisk/arterial and need ED packing/cautery.
Increases severity/recurrence and lowers the threshold for ED.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Uncontrolled bleeding in an anticoagulated patient.

red_flags.description: Haemodynamic compromise — true faintness, near-syncope, large-volume haematemesis

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Failing to instruct lean-forward + soft-cartilage pinch; leaving the patient tilting back and pinching the bony bridge perpetuates bleeding and causes blood swallowing.
Not testing whether correct anterior pressure controls the bleed / whether blood still runs down the throat.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct anterior rhinoscopy of Kiesselbach's plexus
Measured blood pressure and heart rate
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Coach correct first aid; ED if not controlled within 20 minutes of correct pressure or any haemodynamic compromise.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing a posterior bleed or an anticoagulated patient; under-appreciating blood loss.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First aid: sit up, lean forward, pinch the soft part of the nose continuously for 10–15 minutes; avoid tilting the head back. Persistent bleeding despite correct technique, posterior bleeding, anticoagulation or haemodynamic instability warrants ED for cautery/packing.
